macro_segments.title: Introduction to Daf 8 and the Second Perek: Textual Transmission and Overview

micro_segments.title: Greeting and structural overview of the daf; discussion of why this daf is short and the role of Tosafot
Scholarly debate over the authenticity of Rashi on Meilah and its implications for Tosafot's interpretive role
Transition to the second perek; overview of how the first perek discussed psulim and status changes, now moving to normative rules about when meilah and korban status change

macro_segments.title: Chatat Ha'Of: Foundation of Tumah Categories and the Principle of Psul vs. Tamei

micro_segments.title: Introduction to chatat ha'of as a paradigm case; review of tumah classification system (av hatum'ah, rishon, sheni, shlishi, revi'i) and the distinction between tamei and nifsal
Definition of tvul yom and michusarei kipurim; explanation of their status as sheni-like and their effect on terumah versus kodashim
The role of shechita and nimlekah in preparing the chatat ha'of for tumah; transition from alive to dead animal
Stages of status change for chatat ha'of: nimlekah and huchshara for tvul yom and michusarei kipurim; linah of the blood as distinct from linah of the meat
Zrikat hadam as the moment when piggul, nosar, and tamei statuses apply; distinction between the clock for blood versus meat

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Analysis of Psul and Tumah: Resolving the Apparent Contradiction

micro_segments.title: Gemara's query: how can tvul yom make kodashim psul but not tamei if he is a sheni who should make it shlishi
Three positions on tvul yom's tumah status: Abba Shaul (rishon for kodashim), Rabbi Meir (sheni, produces shlishi), Chachamim (psul, not tamei)
Raba's resolution: the mishna reflects the d'oraita position (Chachamim's view); debate over rishon vs. sheni is derabbanan only
Conceptual explanation: tvul yom's tumah is so residual (kalush tumah) that it only invalidates, does not transmit further contamination

macro_segments.title: The Question of Meilah After Zrikat Hadam: Why Does the Gemara Distinguish Between Meat Status and Forbidden Use

micro_segments.title: Gemara's question: why does the mishna say there is no meilah after zrikat hadam when the meat is still prohibited for non-kohanim
Rabbi Chanina's answer referencing the case of yotzei: zrikah removes meilah status but does not permit the meat to be eaten

macro_segments.title: Mitzuy of Chatat Ha'Of: Is Squeezing Out the Blood a Meakev Act

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the question of mitzuy chatat ha'of: whether squeezing is required and when exactly it must occur
Rav (via Rav Huna): mitzuy is not meakev; the mishna's reference to hazah marks the status change without requiring mitzuy
Rav Adda bar Ahava (via Rav): mitzuy is meakev; different girsa of the mishna references mitzuy damah specifically
Gemara's textual proof attempt: analysis of ve-hanishar badam yimatze el yesod hamizbeach chatas hi
Tana d'bei Rabbi Yishmael's interpretation: ve-hanishar is conditional (if there is leftover), not a requirement; chatas hi refers to the beginning of the verse (hazah)
Comparison to minchas: the principle of shana alav hakatuv l'akev applied to determine whether mitzuy is meakev
Baraita on piggul by bird offerings: discussion of whether mitzuy is relevant to the moment of piggul
Gemara's resolution through L'atztedin: the baraita discusses two different animals (chatat and olas ha'of) respectively, not requiring mitzuy for chatat status change
Two versions of Rabbi Yishmael in the Tosefta: one says mitzuy is not required, another says shiraym are meakev, leading to debate over bird offerings

macro_segments.title: The Mishna's Systematic Account of Korban Status Changes: Olat Ha'Of and Its Unique Properties

micro_segments.title: Olat ha'of structure: meilah begins at sanctification; after nimlekah becomes fit for tumah; after mitzuy damah enters realm of piggul nosar tamei
Unique feature of olas ha'of: meilah continues throughout (no zrikah change) because the entire animal is burned on the mizbeach, unlike chatat ha'of whose meat is eaten
When meilah ends for olas ha'of: only when the ashes are taken outside the camp (beis ha-deshen); principle that meilah continues until mitzvaso is completed
Parim Hanisrafim and Se'irim Hanisrafim: special cases burned outside the camp; meilah applies throughout until ashes are removed

macro_segments.title: The Mishna's Treatment of Normal Korbanot: Olah, Chatat, Asham, and Zivchei Shalmei Tzibbur

micro_segments.title: Olah (large animal): meilah from sanctification; shechita allows tumah; after zrikat hadam, no meilah on meat but continues on imurin
Exception for the hide of olah: no meilah after zrikah because kohanim receive it; skin is removed from korban use
Chatat and asham as kodshei kodashim: meat can be eaten (unlike olah), so no meilah on meat after zrikah; meilah continues on imurin
Zivchei shalmei tzibbur: additional instance of kodshei kodashim eaten by kohanim; same rules apply as chatat and asham

macro_segments.title: Non-Flesh Korbanos: Shtei Halechem and Lechem Hapanim—When Kedusha and Status Change Apply

micro_segments.title: Shtei halechem (two loaves at Shavuot): meilah from sanctification of flour; becomes fit for tumah when crust forms (kermu batanur); status tied to sacrificial lambs
Key principle: kedushas haguf (intrinsic sanctity) for bread begins at crust formation, not at raw flour stage; parallel to shechita for animals
Lechem hapanim (showbread): meilah from sanctification; fit for tumah when crust forms; status changes when placed on table (haseder al gavei hashulchan)
No meilah on consumed portions: both shtei halechem and lechem hapanim lose meilah after they become fit to eat (kohanim consume them)

macro_segments.title: Minchos (Meal Offerings): Kedusha Begins at Preparation, Status Depends on Stage of Avodah

micro_segments.title: Minchos structure: meilah from sanctification; fit for tumah when placed in sacred vessel (kadeshu b'kli sharet); linah applies because offering must be brought the same day
Piggul nosar tamei apply after kometz is offered (karev hakometz); the kometz is burned and retains meilah until ashes are removed
No meilah on shirayim (remnant eaten by kohanim); meilah continues on kometz following the rule that meilah ends when food is eaten but continues on burned portions

macro_segments.title: When Does Meilah End: The Question of Ashes and the Role of Trumas Hadeshen

micro_segments.title: Amoraim debate on ashes in the mound (tapuach): does meilah continue on ashes that remain on the mizbeach before trumas hadeshen
Rav's position: no meilah on ashes after trumas hadeshen because the mitzvah is completed; trumas hadeshen is the final avodah
Rabbi Yochanan's position: meilah continues until ashes are taken completely outside the camp because priestly garments are required for removal, making it avodah
Gemara's challenge to Rav: the mishna states meilah applies ad sheteitzei leveit hadeshen, supporting Rabbi Yochanan; discussion of the phrase ad shehei reuia leveit hadeshen

topical_tags.term: tumah classification (av, rishon, sheni, shlishi, revi'i)
kedusha and status change (psulo kodem shechita paradigm)
beis ha-deshen (place where ashes are taken outside)
mitzvaso is completed (principle of when status ends)
shirayim (remainder of mincha eaten by kohanim)
shana alav hakatuv l'akev (principle of repetition making act required)
